active.description: Flags rows belonging to a duplicated combination of columns expected to together form a unique (composite) business key. The specific columns/table are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Duplicate Composite Key
active.reason: PRODUCT_ID is fully distinct so no composite key duplication is likely, but keeping it is cheap protection.

active.description: Flags duplicate non-null values in a column expected to be unique (e.g. a primary/business key). The specific column/table and why it matters are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Uniqueness Violation
active.reason: PRODUCT_ID should remain unique as the business key and this guards it.

active.description: Flags rows where a column expected to hold a strictly positive measure (price, quantity, amount) is zero or negative. The specific column/table and why it matters are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Non-Positive Value
active.reason: PRICE should be strictly positive and STOCK_QTY has zeros, making non-positive detection relevant.

active.description: Flags rows whose column value falls outside an explicit accepted-values list grounded in the column's actually-observed data. The specific column/table/accepted values are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Value Outside Accepted Set
active.reason: CATEGORY has a small stable set of 5 values ideal for accepted-set enforcement.

active.description: Flags rows whose foreign-key-shaped column has no matching row in the referenced table/column. The specific tables/columns and observed orphan rate are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Referential Integrity Violation
active.reason: SUPPLIER_ID is a foreign key that should reference a suppliers table.

active.description: Flags rows where a column expected to always be populated is NULL. The specific column/table and why it matters are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Not-Null Constraint Violation
active.reason: Multiple columns have meaningful null percentages worth flagging.

active.description: Flags a table whose most recent timestamp/date column value is older than an expected freshness threshold. The specific column/table/threshold are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Stale Data / Freshness Violation
active.reason: LAST_UPDATED enables freshness monitoring which is relevant for a mutable catalog.

active.description: Columns ending in _DATE, _DT, or _DAY should use DATE or TIMESTAMP. Storing them as other types prevents date arithmetic and proper sorting.
active.name: Column Type Mismatch — Date column should be DATE or TIMESTAMP
active.reason: LAST_UPDATED is already TIMESTAMP_NTZ so this is satisfied, but keeping it is harmless.

active.description: Production tables should track when rows were inserted via a column such as CREATED_AT, CREATE_DATE, or INSERT_TS. This enables auditing, incremental loads, and change tracking.
active.name: Missing Row Creation Timestamp
active.reason: No creation timestamp column exists, so this legitimately flags a gap.

active.description: Primary key and identifier columns should never be NULL. A nullable PK column breaks referential integrity and causes unexpected results in GROUP BY, JOIN, and deduplication.
active.name: Nullable ID / Primary Key Column
active.reason: PRODUCT_ID is nullable at the schema level and should be a non-null primary key.

active.description: Columns whose names suggest a boolean or flag value (_FL, _FLAG, _IND, IS_, _YN) are stored as VARCHAR. This allows invalid values and prevents efficient filtering. Use BOOLEAN or a small integer type.
active.name: Boolean/Flag Column Stored as VARCHAR
active.reason: IS_ACTIVE is already BOOLEAN so this is satisfied, but low-cost to retain.

active.description: Mutable tables should track the last modification time via UPDATED_AT, MODIFIED_DATE, or equivalent. Required for CDC, incremental ETL, and auditing.
active.name: Missing Row Updated Timestamp
active.reason: LAST_UPDATED can serve as the updated timestamp, though the naming is non-standard so keeping it documents intent.

active.description: Columns ending in '_ID' typically reference another table. Add an unenforced REFERENCES clause for documentation and data lineage tools.
active.name: Foreign Key Column Without FK Constraint
active.reason: SUPPLIER_ID and PRODUCT_ID lack FK constraints that this documents.

active.description: Columns should have descriptive comments explaining their data
active.name: Missing Column Comment
active.reason: Columns appear undocumented so missing-comment detection is valid.

active.description: Tables should have descriptive comments explaining their purpose
active.name: Missing Table Comment
active.reason: Table comment appears absent so this is a valid documentation check.

active.description: Column names like COL1, DATA, VALUE, FIELD, or MISC provide no semantic context. Rename them to describe what they actually store.
active.name: Generic / Uninformative Column Name
active.reason: CATEGORY value 'MISC' and the generic pattern warrant checking for uninformative naming, though column names are mostly descriptive.

active.description: Column names should follow a single naming convention throughout a table (e.g. all UPPER_SNAKE_CASE). Mixing styles makes queries harder to write and datasets harder to join.
active.name: Inconsistent Column Naming Style
active.reason: All columns follow UPPER_SNAKE_CASE consistently, so this rarely fires but is cheap to retain.

active.description: Flags rows whose column value falls outside an explicit accepted-values list grounded in the column's actually-observed data. The specific column/table/accepted values are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Value Outside Accepted Set
active.reason: CATEGORY has only 5 stable observed values; enforcing this accepted set catches future typos or unexpected categories in the product dimension.

active.description: Flags rows where a column expected to hold a strictly positive measure (price, quantity, amount) is zero or negative. The specific column/table and why it matters are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Non-Positive Value
active.reason: PRICE is a monetary measure that should always be strictly positive; a zero or negative price indicates a data-entry or pricing error.

active.description: Flags rows where a column expected to always be populated is NULL. The specific column/table and why it matters are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Not-Null Constraint Violation
active.reason: A product dimension row without a name is unusable for reporting and downstream joins to product displays.
active.violation_evidence: PRODUCT_NAME has null%=15.0, meaning 3 of 20 products have no name.

active.description: Flags rows whose foreign-key-shaped column has no matching row in the referenced table/column. The specific tables/columns and observed orphan rate are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Referential Integrity Violation
active.reason: SUPPLIER_ID is a foreign key with only 5 distinct values and 15% nulls; verifying each references a real supplier prevents orphaned product-to-supplier links.

active.description: Flags duplicate non-null values in a column expected to be unique (e.g. a primary/business key). The specific column/table and why it matters are recorded on each instance.
active.name: Uniqueness Violation
active.reason: PRODUCT_ID is the business key (distinct=20, null%=0) and must stay unique to guarantee reliable joins from fact tables.

skipped.description: Flags rows where a column value does not match a required regular-expression pattern (phone number, code format, etc.). The specific column/table/pattern are recorded on each instance.
skipped.name: Pattern Mismatch
skipped.reason: No column here has a defined regex-style format to enforce, so a generic pattern check is not meaningful.

skipped.description: Flags rows where a column expected to hold an email address does not match a valid email pattern. The specific column/table is recorded on each instance.
skipped.name: Invalid Email Format
skipped.reason: There is no email-shaped column in this table.

skipped.description: Tables should have an assigned owner for accountability
skipped.name: Missing Table Owner
skipped.reason: The table already has an owner (PLAYGROUND_FR_ENGR_ROLE), so this cannot fire.

skipped.description: Columns whose names suggest personally identifiable information (e.g. EMAIL, SSN, PHONE, PASSWORD, DOB, SALARY) should have a Snowflake Dynamic Data Masking policy applied and a PII tag attached.
skipped.name: Potential PII Column Without Masking Policy
skipped.reason: No PII-shaped columns exist in this product catalog.

skipped.description: Columns whose names suggest a date or timestamp are stored as VARCHAR. This prevents date arithmetic, sorting, filtering, and indexing. Convert to DATE or TIMESTAMP.
skipped.name: Date/Timestamp Column Stored as VARCHAR
skipped.reason: LAST_UPDATED is already a proper TIMESTAMP_NTZ, not a VARCHAR.

skipped.description: Tables with more than 50 columns often indicate poor normalisation, merged business entities, or accumulated technical debt. Consider decomposing into focused, related tables.
skipped.name: Table Has Too Many Columns
skipped.reason: The table has only 8 columns, far under any threshold.

skipped.description: Columns ending in _ID, _KEY, _FK, or _PK should use a numeric type (NUMBER, INTEGER, BIGINT). Storing them as VARCHAR causes implicit conversions and silent join failures.
skipped.name: Column Type Mismatch — ID/key column should be numeric
skipped.reason: All ID columns (PRODUCT_ID, SUPPLIER_ID) are already NUMBER.

skipped.description: No column matching common primary-key naming patterns (ID, *_ID, PK_*, *_PK, *_KEY, *_SEQ) was found. Tables without a primary key risk duplicate rows and make joins, deduplication, and CDC harder.
skipped.name: Table May Be Missing a Primary Key
skipped.reason: PRODUCT_ID clearly matches the primary-key naming pattern so this cannot fire.

output.table_type_reason: PRODUCT_CATALOG holds descriptive product attributes keyed by PRODUCT_ID, characteristic of a product dimension/reference table.
